subject: ap-chemistry
title: Intermolecular Forces & Physical Properties
stimulus:
To determine the molar mass of an unknown metal, M, a student reacts iodine with an excess of the metal to
form the water-soluble compound MI
, as represented by the equation above. The reaction proceeds until all
of the I
is consumed. The MI
(aq) solution is quantitatively collected and heated to remove the water, and the
product is dried and weighed to constant mass. The experimental steps are represented below, followed by a
data table.
Data for Unknown Metal Lab
Mass of beaker 125.457 g
Mass of beaker + metal M 126.549 g
Mass of beaker + metal M + I
127.570 g
Mass of MI
, first weighing 1.284 g
Mass of MI
, second weighing 1.284 g

parts.prompt:
Given that the metal M is in excess, calculate the number of moles of I
that reacted.

scoring_points.description: Calculate the correct value with setup and answer

required_elements: Correct mathematical setup or formula application
Correct numerical answer with appropriate sig figs and units

parts.prompt:
Calculate the molar mass of the unknown metal M.
The student hypothesizes that the compound formed in the synthesis reaction is ionic.

scoring_points.description: Calculate the correct value with setup and answer

required_elements: Correct mathematical setup or formula application
Correct numerical answer with appropriate sig figs and units

parts.prompt:
Propose an experimental test the student could perform that could be used to support the hypothesis. Explain
how the results of the test would support the hypothesis if the substance was ionic.
The student hypothesizes that Br
will react with metal M more vigorously than I
did because Br
is a liquid
at room temperature.

scoring_points.description: State the correct answer with a valid chemical justification

required_elements: Valid justification based on chemical principles

common_traps: Stating answer without connecting to the underlying chemical principle

parts.prompt:
Explain why I
is a solid at room temperature whereas Br
is a liquid. Your explanation should clearly
reference the types and relative strengths of the intermolecular forces present in each substance.
While cleaning up after the experiment, the student wishes to dispose of the unused solid I
in a responsible
−
manner. The student decides to convert the solid I
to I (aq) anion. The student has access to three solutions,
$H_2O$(aq), Na
S
O
(aq), and Na
S
O
(aq), and the standard reduction table shown below.

parts.prompt:
Which solution should the student add to $I_2$(s) to reduce it to I (aq)? Circle your answer below. Justify
your answer, including a calculation of E° for the overall reaction.
$H_2O$(aq) Na S O (aq) Na S O (aq)
2 2 2 2 3 2 4 6

scoring_points.description: State the correct answer with a valid chemical justification

required_elements: Valid justification based on chemical principles

common_traps: Stating answer without connecting to the underlying chemical principle

parts.prompt:
Write the balanced net-ionic equation for the reaction between I
and the solution you selected in part (e).
C H OH(aq) + $H_2O$(l) ← → C $H_2O$ − (aq) + $H_2O$+(aq) K = 1.12 × 10$^{-10}$
